network.overall_strategy: The prebiotic synthesis network for threonine is constructed by integrating hydrothermal CO2 reduction and Fischer-Tropsch-type chemistry, surface Strecker and cyanosulfidic pathways, and biochemical assembly via peptide-relevant processes. The network features multiple convergent routes through α-keto acids (pyruvate, glyoxylate), aminonitrile intermediates, aldehydes (acetaldehyde, glycolaldehyde), and HCN. Environmental interconnection is realized by the transfer of hydrothermal organics to wet-dry surface pools for further C-N formation and then to biochemical compartmentalization for hydrolysis and peptide assembly. Ten pathways to threonine are realized, each passing through at least one of the hub molecules: pyruvate, acetaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, HCN, and threonine aminonitrile.

reactions.name: CO2 reduction to formate/acetate
reactions.mechanism: CO2 is reduced by H2 over magnetite/pyrite surfaces to acetate (part of Fischer–Tropsch-type process).
reactions.reasoning: Supported by hydrothermal vent experiments and Fischer–Tropsch-type reducing systems (He et al. 2010).

reactions.name: Acetate and CO2 to pyruvate
reactions.mechanism: Stepwise carboxylation and reduction of acetate with CO2 and H2 to pyruvate on greigite surfaces.
reactions.reasoning: In line with (Huber & Wächtershäuser, 1997; Cody et al., 2000) for prebiotic pyruvate synthesis via carbon fixation on FeS/Greigite.

reactions.name: Pyruvate to acetaldehyde via decarboxylation
reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate undergoes mineral-catalyzed decarboxylation to acetaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Supported by Huber & Wächtershäuser, 1997 for FeS-catalyzed decarboxylation of keto acids.

reactions.name: Aldol condensation: glycolaldehyde formation
reactions.mechanism: Glycolic acid (hydrothermal) dehydrated and concentrated on mineral surfaces to glycolaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Borate stabilizes sugars; see (Kim et al., 2011) and (Benner et al., 2012).

reactions.name: Formose reaction: formaldehyde to glycolaldehyde
reactions.mechanism: Successive aldol reactions form glycolaldehyde from formaldehyde under stabilization by borate minerals.
reactions.reasoning: Formose and aldol chemistry on borate minerals is well-established (Benner et al., 2012).

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis: threonine aminonitrile formation from lactaldehyde
reactions.mechanism: Aldehyde + HCN + NH3 react on clay/UV surfaces to yield aminonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Strecker amino acid pathways on clays and with surface UV catalysis are robust (Danger et al., 2012; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Aldol condensation: acetaldehyde and glycolaldehyde to lactaldehyde
reactions.mechanism: Mineral-catalyzed condensation of acetaldehyde and glycolaldehyde generates lactaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Prebiotic aldol condensations on clays are key C-C bond forming steps (Lambert, 2008).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of threonine aminonitrile to threonine
reactions.mechanism: Hydration and hydrolysis of aminonitrile converts nitrile to amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Aminonitrile hydrolysis to amino acids is robust under mild/neutral conditions (Powner et al., 2009).

reactions.name: Alternative Strecker: Glycolaldehyde + acetaldehyde + HCN + NH3
reactions.mechanism: Direct Strecker amino acid synthesis with geminal-diol intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: Multiple aldehyde Strecker pathways plausible on clay minerals (Sharma et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type hydrothermal route to acetaldehyde and ethanol
reactions.mechanism: FTT reactions yield C2–C3 organics including acetaldehyde, ethanol.

reactions.name: Transamination of 3-hydroxybutanal (from glycolaldehyde+acetaldehyde) with NH3 to aminonitrile
reactions.mechanism: Aldol product amination and cyanation lead to threonine aminonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Extends classic Strecker mechanism; surface clays facilitate amination and nucleophilic attack (Danger et al., 2012).

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-Formose-Canonical Strecker
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 to acetate to pyruvate, hydrothermal/surface conversion to glycolaldehyde via formose/aldol, then canonical Strecker to threonine aminonitrile and hydrolysis to threonine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal Pyruvate-Derived Aldol/Transamination
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate conversion to acetaldehyde, surface aldol condensation with glycolaldehyde, followed by Strecker to aminonitrile and hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Fischer-Tropsch Acetaldehyde-Glycolaldehyde Route
pathways.description: CO2 conversion via FTT yields acetaldehyde, surface reactions form lactaldehyde, Strecker synthesis follows.

pathways.name: Direct Formose-Glycolaldehyde Dimerization Pathway
pathways.description: Formose chemistry directly produces glycolaldehyde, which dimers to lactaldehyde and enters Strecker synthesis without passing through hydrothermal acetaldehyde.

pathways.name: Mixed-Aldehyde Strecker Pathway
pathways.description: Surface glycolaldehyde and hydrothermal (or surface) acetaldehyde, plus HCN, NH3, directly yield threonine aminonitrile via expanded Strecker.

pathways.name: 3-Hydroxybutanal Route
pathways.description: Aldol condensation of glycolaldehyde and acetaldehyde gives 3-hydroxybutanal, followed by transamination and hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-Surface Cross-Route (Acetate & Glycolic Acid)
pathways.description: Hydrothermal acetate/pyruvate to glycolic acid, surface conversion to glycolaldehyde, then to aminonitrile.

pathways.name: Alternative FTT-Aldol Pathway
pathways.description: Fischer-Tropsch emission of acetaldehyde/ethanol, acetaldehyde combines with glycolaldehyde from formose, via aldol and Strecker.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-Glycolic Surface Loop
pathways.description: Acetate is converted to glycolic acid in the hydrothermal environment, which on the surface forms glycolaldehyde, then onward via lactaldehyde.

pathways.name: Direct Glycolaldehyde-Driven Strecker
pathways.description: Glycolaldehyde (from surface or hydrothermal flow) reacts directly in an extended surface Strecker process producing aminonitrile.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Classic three-environment pathway from CO2 through formose and Strecker synthesis. Each step is literature-aligned and plausible; environmental transitions and intermediates are realistic and well-chosen.
suggestions: Specify minerals/catalysts in key surface reactions (e.g., borates, clay)

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Pyruvate-derived pathway is chemically robust; decarboxylation and subsequent aldol chemistry are supported by literature. Pathway is convergent and efficiently reaches the aminonitrile intermediate.
Fischer-Tropsch-type (FTT) synthesis is plausible (hydrothermal), but higher complexity in environmental transition. Aldehyde to aminonitrile via mixed Strecker is chemically sensible.
suggestions: Cite FTT references for acetaldehyde/ethanol selectivity; clarify yields

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Dimerization of glycolaldehyde to larger sugars/intermediates is less evidenced, but plausible. The subsequent Strecker/hydrolysis steps are solid.
suggestions: Cite support for glycolaldehyde dimerization under prebiotic conditions

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Mixed-aldehyde Strecker is well-supported, provides short route. Appropriate use of hub molecules. Hydrolytic final step is robust.
suggestions: Clarify ratio/depletion issues with mixed aldehydes in prebiotic pools

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Uses less common 3-hydroxybutanal intermediate; formation is plausible under aldol conditions. Transamination is hypothetical but reasonable.
suggestions: More detail on transamination step – is prebiotic aminonitrile formation from this intermediate demonstrated?

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Good integration of acetate/glycolic acid loop. Steps are known prebiotic routes; transitions are well-supported. Slight ambiguity in minor organic pools but overall excellent.
suggestions: Diagram would help clarify glycolic acid role in surface processes

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Well-rationalized alternative FTT-aldol-Strecker route. All steps plausible; smooth environmental integration.
Glycolic surface loop is slightly more hypothetical but steps are within plausible prebiotic chemistry. Reasonably convergent.
Direct glycolaldehyde Strecker is shortest and efficient, though dependent on local aldehyde abundance. Chemically valid sequence.

evaluation.network_feedback: This network is excellent, with robust topology (15 well-connected molecule nodes, all hub molecules have high degree, no isolated subgraphs). Environmental progression is strongly justified: hydrothermal reactions feed surface Strecker chemistry, which transitions to biochemical (hydrolytic) assembly. Every reaction is chemically sound with minor caveats for surface intermediates (e.g., 3-hydroxybutanal, glycolaldehyde dimerization) but otherwise aligns with literature precedent. Multiple convergent, redundant pathways raise confidence and robustness. Hub selection and utilization are strong (pyruvate, glycolaldehyde, threonine aminonitrile, HCN, acetaldehyde all act as core connection points). Ten reasonable, partly overlapping yet distinct routes mean network redundancy is excellent and failure of a single path does not collapse synthesis. Suggestions mainly concern minor citation and mechanistic detail. Overall, this network is among the best examples of an integrated prebiotic synthesis strategy.
